claims.text: A leaked government document named Project Anchor claims Earth's gravity will briefly turn off on August 12, 2026.

evidence.quote:
According to a leaked
government document, this happens on
August 12th, 2026. NASA calls it Project
Anchor

claims.rationale: Host opens with this claim as the viral document's core assertion, then in the verdict section explicitly calls Project Anchor a hoax originating on TikTok/Instagram. Near-zero directTruth because the host (and physics) decisively debunks it.

claims.text: The Project Anchor document is a hoax that first appeared on TikTok and Instagram in late December 2025.

evidence.quote:
Project Anchor is a hoax. The leaked
document first appeared on Tik Tok and
Instagram in late December 2025

claims.rationale: Host's explicit verdict; matches NASA's real December-2025 public rebuttal of the viral document. Verifiable claim about a specific, datable social-media origin.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Direct debunking of the leaked-document claim: it is pure social-media fiction, not a real classified document.

claims.text: Gravitational waves are ripples in spacetime and cannot intersect near Earth in a way that would cancel gravity.

evidence.quote:
NASA has never
monitored gravitational waves that could
intersect near Earth.

claims.rationale: Host accurately describes the physics of gravitational waves (spacetime ripples, not cancelable force beams). Matches textbook general relativity and LIGO documentation.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] The physics mechanism posited by the hoax document (wave interference canceling Earth's gravity) is impossible.

claims.text: Earth can only suddenly lose its gravity if it suddenly loses its mass.

evidence.quote:
The only way
Earth could suddenly lose its gravity
would be if the planet suddenly lost its
mass

claims.rationale: Direct consequence of Newton's F=GMm/r^2 and general relativity (gravity sourced by mass-energy). Well-established physics.

dependencies.rationale: [logical] The hoax requires gravity to vanish without mass loss, which violates the defining relation between mass and gravity.

claims.text: Isaac Newton published the laws of universal gravitation in 1687 without explaining what gravity actually is.

evidence.quote:
Newton figured out how it
behaves in 1687

claims.rationale: Well-documented historical fact: Principia Mathematica published 1687, contains universal gravitation. Newton's hypotheses non fingo line about mechanism is accurately summarized.

claims.text: Einstein's 1915 general theory of relativity redefined gravity as the warping of spacetime by mass.

evidence.quote:
Albert Einstein
took the next step in 1915. His general
theory of relativity redefined gravity.
Mass warps spaceime

claims.rationale: Standard physics history: GR published November 1915. Mass warping spacetime is the mainstream colloquial summary.

claims.text: The graviton — the hypothesized carrier particle of gravity — has never been detected.

evidence.quote:
graviton. But the
graviton has never been detected, not
even once. Freeman Dyson

claims.rationale: Mainstream physics consensus as of 2026: no experimental confirmation of individual gravitons. Dyson argument about detector collapsing to black hole is legitimate.

claims.text: Gravity is approximately 10^40 times weaker than the electromagnetic force — known in physics as the hierarchy problem.

evidence.quote:
It's 10 to the
40th power weaker than electromagnetism

claims.rationale: Correctly cites the hierarchy-problem ratio (~10^40 between electromagnetism and gravity between two protons). Well-known physics puzzle.

claims.text: Thomas Townsend Brown's electrogravidics research drew US-military interest in the 1950s before going classified in 1956.

evidence.quote:
Brown had caught the attention of the US
military

glas
Aircraft, Glenn Martin, General
Electric, Bell Aircraft, Convey, Lear,
Spirand, all of them had
electrogravidics programs

claims.rationale: Thomas Townsend Brown's biphase Townsend-effect work and the 1956 Aviation Week article on anti-gravity industry research are documented. The claim that the field 'went dark' post-1956 is partly true (public publications declined) but partly an overinterpretation.

claims.text: In 1992 Russian physicist Eugene Podkletnov claimed a rotating superconducting disc could reduce the weight of objects above it by about 2%.

evidence.quote:
Russian physicist Eugene
Padletnovv made headlines. He claimed
that a rotating superconducting disc
could reduce the weight

claims.rationale: Podkletnov's 1992 paper and Sunday Telegraph headline are real; however the ~2% weight reduction has never been independently replicated despite multiple attempts. Claim-as-reported is historically accurate but the physical effect remains unconfirmed.

claims.text: Dr. Ning Li received a $450,000 DoD grant in 2001, founded AC Gravity LLC, obtained top-secret clearance, and stopped publishing afterward.

evidence.quote:
2001, she
received a $450,000
grant from the Department of Defense.
Then she founded a company called AC
Gravity LLC and she obta

claims.rationale: Ning Li's DoD funding, AC Gravity LLC, and publication gap are documented in open sources. Host frames as sinister; the classified-work interpretation is speculation but the factual scaffold is verifiable.

claims.text: Bob Lazar claimed he helped reverse-engineer alien spacecraft at S4 near Area 51 and that their propulsion used element 115.

evidence.quote:
ty called S4 [music] near Area 51
in the Nevada desert.
He said he helped reverse engineer alien
spacecraft. Lazar said the propulsion
system used element 115

claims.rationale: Lazar did make these public claims (1989 KLAS interview) — that part is historical fact. Truth of the claims themselves (alien craft, element 115 fuel) is highly contested: element 115 (moscovium) does exist but is millisecond-lived, which host flags.

claims.text: A country that successfully develops anti-gravity technology would gain an overwhelming military advantage that justifies long-term classification.

evidence.quote:
country that cracks anti-gravity doesn't
just win wars. It makes war obsolete.
Well, obso

claims.rationale: Host's geopolitical argument for why anti-gravity research would stay classified. Speculative — no existing anti-gravity tech has been demonstrated; the 'makes war obsolete' framing is rhetorical. Omitting directTruth.

claims.text: Scientifically we still do not know what gravity fundamentally is.
claims.rationale: Supported by mainstream physics: we have excellent descriptive theories (GR, quantum mechanics) but no unified quantum theory of gravity and no direct graviton observation. Host's framing is an accurate summary of that open problem.

dependencies.rationale: Graviton non-detection is a concrete manifestation of the broader 'we don't know what gravity is' gap.
